title: Data Classes
description: The ultimate shortcut for holding information.

slides.title: Boring Containers 📦
slides.content:
## Holding Data

Often, we build classes that do absolutely no 'work'. They don't have member functions like `bark()` or `drive()`. 

They exist purely to hold data together (e.g., `class User(val name: String, val age: Int)`).

slides.title: The Java Nightmare ☕
slides.content: In Java, standard data-holder classes were miserable. If you wanted to cleanly `.print()` the object, compare two of them using `==`, or cleanly `.copy()` them, you had to write 50+ lines of miserable, ugly backend code inside the class yourself manually.

slides.title: Kotlin's Silver Bullet 🪙
slides.content:
Kotlin solves this with a single word: `data`.

By typing `data class`, Kotlin silently auto-generates all 50 lines of that required utility code perfectly behind the scenes for you in half a second.

quizData.question: What happens functionally when you add the `data` keyword to a class definition?

options.text: It automatically generates immensely helpful utility functions like cleanly printing (`toString()`) and accurate data equality (`==`) instantly.

quizData.explanation: The `data` keyword is Kotlin's greatest shortcut. It saves millions of hours globally by auto-writing tedious data management infrastructures effortlessly within milliseconds.
